subject: Medicine
stem: A 55-year-old male presents with crushing chest pain radiating to the left arm for 2 hours. ECG shows ST elevation in leads V1-V4 with reciprocal ST depression in leads II, III, and aVF. Which coronary artery is most likely occluded?
explanation: ST elevation in V1-V4 (anterior leads) with reciprocal changes in II, III, aVF is the classic pattern of anterior STEMI caused by LAD occlusion. The LAD supplies the anterior wall and septum. Reciprocal depression in inferior leads is a hallmark of anterior STEMI. RCA occlusion causes inferior STEMI (II, III, aVF elevation). LCx causes lateral STEMI (I, aVL, V5, V6). Left main occlusion is usually catastrophic and causes diffuse changes.
highYield: Anterior STEMI = LAD occlusion = ST elevation V1-V4 + reciprocal depression II/III/aVF. This is the most tested territory in NEET PG.
pyqNote: Appeared in NEET PG 2019, 2021 - ECG territory identification is a high-yield topic.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 62-year-old hypertensive male presents with inferior STEMI (ST elevation in II, III, aVF). ST elevation is more prominent in lead III than lead II, and there is ST depression in lead I. Which artery is most likely culprit?
explanation: Inferior STEMI is caused by RCA in 80% of cases and LCx in 20%. When ST elevation is greater in III than II, and lead I shows ST depression (or aVL shows reciprocal changes), RCA is the culprit. When ST elevation is more prominent in II than III with positive aVL, LCx is more likely. RCA supplies the inferior wall, posterior wall, and often the RV. Standard teaching: III>II = RCA; II>III = LCx.
highYield: RCA causes 80% of inferior STEMIs. III > II elevation with aVL/I reciprocal depression = RCA. II > III = LCx.
pyqNote: NEET PG frequently tests differentiation between RCA vs LCx in inferior STEMI using lead comparison.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 50-year-old male presents with chest pain. ECG shows tall R waves in V1-V2, ST depression in V1-V3, and upright tall T waves in V1-V2. No ST elevation is seen. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Posterior STEMI is a mirror image (reciprocal) of the true posterior wall infarction. Classic findings: tall R waves in V1-V2 (reciprocal of posterior Q waves), ST depression in V1-V3 (reciprocal of posterior ST elevation), and upright tall T waves (reciprocal of posterior T inversion). Placing leads on the posterior chest (V7-V9) would show frank ST elevation. This pattern is caused by LCx or RCA occlusion (posterior branches). It is critical not to miss as there is NO direct ST elevation in standard leads.
highYield: Posterior STEMI = tall R + ST depression + tall upright T in V1-V3. All findings are RECIPROCAL of true posterior elevation. Apply posterior leads V7-V9 to confirm.
pyqNote: Posterior MI is a classic NEET PG trap question - absence of ST elevation does not rule out STEMI.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 58-year-old male presents with inferior STEMI. He develops hypotension, raised JVP, and clear lung fields (Kussmaul sign positive). ECG shows ST elevation in lead V4R. What is the most appropriate initial management?
explanation: ST elevation in V4R (right-sided lead) in the context of inferior STEMI indicates right ventricular infarction. The triad of hypotension + elevated JVP + clear lung fields (no pulmonary congestion) is pathognomonic of RV infarct. The RV is preload dependent; therefore, the cornerstone of management is IV fluid challenge to augment RV filling. Nitrates and diuretics are CONTRAINDICATED as they reduce preload and precipitate cardiovascular collapse. If fluids fail, dobutamine can be added.
highYield: RV infarct = V4R ST elevation + hypotension + raised JVP + clear lungs. Treatment = IV fluids. Nitrates/diuretics are contraindicated.
pyqNote: RV infarct management is a repeat NEET PG question. The contraindication of nitrates is the most tested fact.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 48-year-old female presents with acute chest pain. ECG shows ST elevation in leads I, aVL, V5, and V6 with reciprocal ST depression in leads II, III, and aVF. Which artery is most likely occluded?
explanation: ST elevation in I, aVL, V5, V6 (lateral leads) represents lateral STEMI, caused by occlusion of the Left Circumflex Artery (LCx). The LCx supplies the lateral wall of the left ventricle. Reciprocal changes in inferior leads (II, III, aVF) are typically seen. RCA causes inferior STEMI. LAD proximal occlusion causes anterior STEMI (V1-V4). The Posterior Descending Artery (PDA) is a branch of RCA in 80% of cases.
highYield: Lateral STEMI = I + aVL + V5 + V6 elevation = LCx occlusion. Reciprocal changes in inferior leads.
pyqNote: Territory identification is a standard NEET PG question format; lateral territory is commonly tested.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 45-year-old male presents to the ER with chest pain that has now resolved. He appears comfortable. ECG shows deep, symmetric T-wave inversions in V2 and V3. Troponin is mildly elevated. He is about to be discharged as 'low risk.' What is the most critical next step?
options: Admit and perform urgent coronary angiography
Give aspirin and repeat ECG in 24 hours
Perform exercise treadmill test to risk-stratify
explanation: This ECG pattern (deep symmetric T-wave inversions in V2-V3, pain-free state, mild troponin rise) is classic Wellens syndrome Type B, indicating critical proximal LAD stenosis. Despite the patient appearing comfortable, this pattern represents a 'pre-infarction' state and is a harbinger of massive anterior MI. Stress testing is ABSOLUTELY CONTRAINDICATED as it can precipitate complete occlusion. Urgent coronary angiography with likely PCI is required. Discharging this patient is a critical error.
highYield: Wellens Type A = biphasic T (positive-negative) V2-V3; Type B = deep symmetric T inversion V2-V3. Both = proximal LAD critical stenosis. Stress test CONTRAINDICATED.
pyqNote: Wellens syndrome is an emerging high-yield NEET PG topic - the contraindication of stress test is the most tested danger point.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 52-year-old male with acute chest pain shows the following ECG pattern: upsloping ST-segment depression at the J-point in V1-V6, followed by tall, symmetrical, peaked T waves. There is no frank ST elevation. Which diagnosis best explains this pattern and what is the management?
options: Proximal LAD occlusion - treat as STEMI equivalent with urgent PCI
explanation: De Winter T-wave pattern (upsloping ST depression at J-point + tall symmetric T waves in precordial leads, particularly V1-V6) is a STEMI-equivalent representing acute proximal LAD occlusion. It occurs in approximately 2% of anterior MIs. Critically, there is NO classic ST elevation, so it is easily missed. This pattern does NOT evolve into classic STEMI but still represents complete or near-complete LAD occlusion. Management is identical to STEMI: urgent primary PCI. Hyperkalemia causes peaked T waves but without upsloping ST depression.
highYield: De Winter T-waves = upsloping ST depression + tall symmetric T waves in V1-V6 = proximal LAD occlusion = STEMI equivalent. Treat with urgent PCI.
pyqNote: De Winter pattern is a newer topic increasingly appearing in PG entrance exams - distinguish from hyperkalemia peaked T waves.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 65-year-old male with known LBBB presents with chest pain. ECG shows: QRS >120ms, broad notched R in V5-V6, QS pattern in V1, and ST elevation of 5mm in lead V2 (concordant with QRS direction). Which statement is correct regarding STEMI diagnosis in LBBB?
options: LBBB always indicates acute MI and needs immediate PCI
Concordant ST elevation ≥1mm in any lead is a Sgarbossa criterion for STEMI
ST elevation in LBBB is always discordant and normal
LBBB with chest pain never requires intervention without biomarkers
explanation: In LBBB, normal repolarization is discordant (ST and T wave go opposite to QRS). Sgarbossa criteria help identify STEMI in LBBB: (1) Concordant ST elevation ≥1mm in any lead (most specific, 5 points); (2) Concordant ST depression ≥1mm in V1-V3 (3 points); (3) Discordant ST elevation ≥5mm (2 points). A score ≥3 is diagnostic of acute MI. New or presumed new LBBB with chest pain is treated as STEMI equivalent. Modified Sgarbossa adds ST/S ratio >0.25 for discordant changes.
highYield: LBBB: QRS >120ms, RSR in V1, QS in V5-V6. Sgarbossa: concordant ST elevation ≥1mm = most specific criterion for STEMI in LBBB.
pyqNote: Sgarbossa criteria are high-yield for NEET PG. The definition of concordant vs discordant change is the key concept.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 70-year-old patient's ECG shows QRS duration of 140ms, a broad monophasic R wave in lead V5 with notching (M-shaped), and a QS pattern in lead V1. Which is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Left Bundle Branch Block (LBBB) features: QRS >120ms (here 140ms), broad monophasic or notched (M-shaped/RSR') R in lateral leads V5-V6 and I, aVL; QS or rS pattern in V1 (no R wave or tiny r wave); absent septal Q waves in lateral leads. RBBB features: RSR' (M-shaped) in V1 (not V5-V6), wide S wave in I, V5-V6. WPW shows short PR + delta wave. VT shows AV dissociation typically.
highYield: LBBB = broad notched R in V5/V6/I/aVL + QS in V1 + QRS >120ms + no septal Q waves in lateral leads.
pyqNote: Basic LBBB recognition is tested in most PG entrance exams. Know the lead distribution.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 28-year-old female presents with sudden-onset palpitations, heart rate of 180 bpm, regular rhythm, narrow QRS complex, and no visible P waves (or pseudo S wave in II/pseudo R' in V1). Vagal maneuvers fail. What is the drug of choice?
explanation: AVNRT (AV Nodal Re-entrant Tachycardia) is the most common cause of regular narrow-complex SVT (paroxysmal SVT). It is caused by a re-entrant circuit within or near the AV node (slow-fast pathway in typical AVNRT). ECG shows: regular narrow complex tachycardia, P waves hidden in or just after QRS (pseudo S in II/pseudo R' in V1). First line: vagal maneuvers. If unsuccessful: IV Adenosine (6mg, then 12mg) which transiently blocks AV node and terminates the re-entry circuit. Success rate >90%. Lidocaine is for VT. Adenosine is the DOC for acute narrow complex SVT.
highYield: AVNRT = most common SVT, re-entry within AV node. ECG: regular narrow QRS, hidden P waves. Rx: Vagal maneuvers → IV Adenosine → IV Verapamil/Diltiazem. Ablation is curative.
pyqNote: AVNRT vs AVRT and adenosine use is repeatedly tested in NEET PG. The pseudo R' in V1 finding is high-yield.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 22-year-old male is found to have a short PR interval of 0.10 seconds, a slurred upstroke of the QRS (delta wave), and a wide QRS of 130ms on routine ECG. He has no symptoms. Which statement about this condition is CORRECT?
options: Digoxin is the first-line treatment for AF in this condition
This pattern is diagnostic of AVNRT
Adenosine should be avoided if pre-excited AF develops
Verapamil is safe and preferred for rate control in this condition
explanation: This ECG pattern (short PR + delta wave + wide QRS) is Wolff-Parkinson-White (WPW) syndrome caused by an accessory pathway (Bundle of Kent) bypassing the AV node. In WPW with pre-excited AF (irregular wide-complex tachycardia with varying QRS morphology), AV nodal blocking agents (Adenosine, Digoxin, Verapamil, Beta-blockers) are ABSOLUTELY CONTRAINDICATED. They block the AV node while leaving the accessory pathway unaffected, leading to unopposed conduction down the accessory pathway, potentially causing VF and sudden cardiac death. Treatment of WPW with AF: IV Procainamide or IV Ibutilide. Definitive treatment: Radiofrequency catheter ablation of accessory pathway.
highYield: WPW = delta wave + short PR + wide QRS. Pre-excited AF: AVOID adenosine/digoxin/verapamil/beta-blockers. Use Procainamide. Definitive = ablation.
pyqNote: WPW contraindications (especially digoxin and verapamil) are high-frequency NEET PG questions.

subject: Medicine
stem: During SVT, ECG shows a regular narrow-complex tachycardia at 190 bpm. P waves are visible after the QRS complex with RP interval >70ms (long RP' tachycardia). Which mechanism is most likely responsible?
explanation: In orthodromic AVRT, conduction goes anterograde through the AV node (narrow QRS) and retrograde through the accessory pathway (hence P wave visible AFTER the QRS). The RP interval in AVRT is typically >70ms (P visible separately after QRS). In typical AVNRT (slow-fast), retrograde conduction is very fast, so P waves are buried in or immediately after the QRS (RP <70ms, pseudo S wave in II, pseudo R' in V1). Long RP tachycardia differentials: atypical AVNRT (fast-slow), AVRT, atrial tachycardia. The RP > 70ms and the context point to AVRT.
highYield: Typical AVNRT: short RP (<70ms, P buried). AVRT (orthodromic): long RP (>70ms, P visible after QRS). Both are narrow complex, terminated by adenosine.
pyqNote: RP interval analysis to differentiate SVT types is an advanced NEET PG topic. Know RP >70ms = AVRT or atypical AVNRT.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 60-year-old male with heart failure (EF 32%) is on optimal medical therapy including ramipril and carvedilol. He remains symptomatic (NYHA Class II). What is the next best step to improve mortality?
explanation: For HFrEF (EF <40%), the 4 pillars of mortality-reducing therapy are: ACEi/ARB + Beta-blocker + Mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist (MRA like spironolactone) + SGLT2 inhibitor. If a patient remains symptomatic on ACEi + beta-blocker and is hemodynamically stable (SBP >100, no AKI/hyperkalemia), the ACEi should be REPLACED (not added to) with Sacubitril-Valsartan (ARNI - Angiotensin Receptor Neprilysin Inhibitor) per PARADIGM-HF trial, which showed 20% further reduction in CV death/hospitalization over enalapril. Digoxin reduces hospitalization but NOT mortality.
highYield: HFrEF 4 pillars: ACEi + beta-blocker + spironolactone + SGLT2i. Replace ACEi with sacubitril-valsartan (ARNI) if tolerated = PARADIGM-HF trial. Washout period of 36 hours needed between ACEi and ARNI.
pyqNote: ARNI replacing ACEi in HFrEF is a very high-yield recent addition to NEET PG cardiology.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 55-year-old male with ischemic cardiomyopathy has EF of 30% despite 3 months of optimal medical therapy (ACEi + carvedilol + spironolactone + SGLT2i). ECG shows LBBB with QRS of 150ms. What is the most appropriate next device recommendation?
explanation: This patient meets criteria for BOTH ICD and CRT: EF <35% on optimal medical therapy for >3 months (ICD indication for primary prevention of SCD) AND LBBB with QRS ≥150ms and EF <35% (strongest CRT indication per ESC/ACC guidelines). When both ICD and CRT are indicated, CRT-D (biventricular pacemaker + defibrillator) is recommended. CRT alone (CRT-P) is used when ICD is not indicated or patient is too frail. CRT reduces mortality, hospitalizations, improves symptoms and EF. The QRS ≥150ms with LBBB morphology is the strongest predictor of CRT response.
highYield: ICD: EF <35% on OMT >3 months. CRT: EF <35% + LBBB + QRS ≥150ms. Both = CRT-D. CRT-P if ICD not indicated.
pyqNote: ICD and CRT indications with EF cut-offs are repeated NEET PG topics. The 35% EF threshold is critical.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 72-year-old obese hypertensive female presents with dyspnea on exertion and bilateral pedal edema. Echo shows EF of 58%, LV hypertrophy, and diastolic dysfunction Grade II. BNP is elevated. Which therapy has shown mortality benefit in her condition?
explanation: This patient has HFpEF (EF >50%). Unlike HFrEF, no medication has shown clear mortality benefit in HFpEF for long. However, EMPEROR-Preserved and DELIVER trials showed SGLT2 inhibitors (empagliflozin, dapagliflozin) reduce cardiovascular death and heart failure hospitalization in HFpEF, making them an emerging benefit drug. Current HFpEF management: Diuretics for symptom control (volume overload), control comorbidities (HTN, DM, AF, obesity), SGLT2 inhibitors for emerging mortality/morbidity benefit. ACEi and beta-blockers and spironolactone have NOT shown clear mortality benefit in large HFpEF trials.
highYield: HFpEF (EF >50%): No proven mortality drug until SGLT2i. Diuretics for symptoms. SGLT2i = EMPEROR-Preserved and DELIVER trials show benefit.
pyqNote: HFpEF management is evolving - SGLT2i benefit in HFpEF is a very recent high-yield update.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 65-year-old male presents with acute dyspnea. Chest X-ray shows cardiomegaly and pulmonary congestion. BNP level returns at 820 pg/mL. Which of the following is TRUE regarding BNP in heart failure?
options: BNP >100 pg/mL rules OUT heart failure as a cause of dyspnea
BNP is secreted primarily by atrial myocytes in response to volume overload
BNP >400 pg/mL strongly supports heart failure as the cause of dyspnea
NT-proBNP has a shorter half-life than BNP
explanation: BNP (B-type Natriuretic Peptide) is secreted primarily by ventricular myocytes (not atrial) in response to ventricular wall stress/volume overload. BNP interpretation: <100 pg/mL = heart failure unlikely (high negative predictive value to rule OUT HF); 100-400 pg/mL = indeterminate (consider other causes); >400 pg/mL = heart failure likely. NT-proBNP has a LONGER half-life than BNP (~60-120 min BNP vs ~60-120 min, but NT-proBNP lasts longer and has higher values). Elevated BNP correlates with disease severity and prognosis.
highYield: BNP <100 = rules OUT HF. BNP >400 = rules IN HF. Secreted by ventricles (not atria). NT-proBNP lasts longer, has higher absolute values (cutoff ~300 pg/mL for acute HF).
pyqNote: BNP cutoff values and source (ventricular) are standard NEET PG facts.

subject: Medicine
stem: A patient is being evaluated for heart failure. Which of the following is a MAJOR Framingham criterion for the diagnosis of heart failure?
explanation: Framingham criteria require 2 major OR 1 major + 2 minor criteria for heart failure diagnosis. Major criteria: Paroxysmal Nocturnal Dyspnea (PND), orthopnea, raised JVP, pulmonary rales, cardiomegaly, acute pulmonary edema, S3 gallop, hepatojugular reflux, weight loss >4.5kg in 5 days on treatment. Minor criteria: bilateral ankle edema, nocturnal cough, dyspnea on exertion, hepatomegaly, pleural effusion, reduced vital capacity, tachycardia >120 bpm. PND is a major criterion; bilateral ankle edema, hepatomegaly, and nocturnal cough are minor criteria.
highYield: Major Framingham criteria: PND, orthopnea, raised JVP, S3 gallop, pulmonary rales, cardiomegaly, acute pulmonary edema. Minor: pedal edema, hepatomegaly, nocturnal cough.
pyqNote: Framingham criteria classification (major vs minor) is a standard NEET PG question - PND is commonly mistaken as minor.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 35-year-old female with rheumatic heart disease has a loud S1, opening snap, and a mid-diastolic rumble with presystolic accentuation. She is in sinus rhythm. The opening snap occurs very early after S2. What does the early opening snap signify?
explanation: The A2-OS (Aortic component of S2 to Opening Snap) interval reflects severity of mitral stenosis. In severe MS, LA pressure is very high, causing the mitral valve to snap open rapidly after aortic valve closure → SHORT A2-OS interval (early OS, close to S2). In mild MS, LA pressure is only mildly elevated, so the valve opens later → LONG A2-OS interval. Mnemonic: Severe MS = Short A2-OS. Presystolic accentuation occurs only in sinus rhythm (lost in AF). Malar flush (mitral facies) is a clinical sign. AF is the most common complication of MS.
highYield: MS severity: Short A2-OS = severe; Long A2-OS = mild. Loud S1 + OS + MDM + presystolic accentuation (sinus rhythm only). AF = most common complication.
pyqNote: A2-OS interval and MS severity is a very high-yield NEET PG question. The inverse relationship must be memorized.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 40-year-old female with severe mitral stenosis (MVA 0.8 cm², NYHA Class III) undergoes echocardiography. Wilkins score is 10. She has no significant MR. What is the most appropriate intervention?
explanation: The Wilkins score (also called the Echo score) assesses MS severity on 4 parameters (each 0-4): leaflet mobility, leaflet thickening, subvalvular thickening, and calcification. Total score 0-16. Wilkins score ≤8: PTMC is appropriate (flexible, pliable valve, likely to respond well). Wilkins score >8 (here = 10): Valve is heavily calcified/thickened/immobile; PTMC is likely to fail or cause severe MR. Hence, Mitral Valve Replacement (MVR) is indicated. PTMC is also contraindicated with significant MR (grade ≥2+), LA thrombus, or severe subvalvular disease.
highYield: Wilkins score ≤8 = PTMC appropriate. Wilkins score >8 = MVR needed. Also contraindicate PTMC: MR ≥2+, LA thrombus.
pyqNote: Wilkins score cutoff of 8 for PTMC vs MVR decision is one of the most frequently tested valvular disease questions in NEET PG.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 55-year-old male with chronic severe mitral regurgitation (MR) is asymptomatic. Echo shows EF of 58% and end-systolic diameter (ESD) of 42mm. What is the appropriate management?
options: Wait until symptoms develop regardless of echo parameters
explanation: In chronic severe MR, surgery is indicated based on symptoms OR echo parameters. Surgery is indicated if: EF <60% (note: EF is overestimated in MR due to low afterload; EF 55-60% = equivalent to EF 30-35% in other conditions) OR ESD >40mm (indicating LV dysfunction), even in asymptomatic patients. Here, EF 58% (<60%) and ESD 42mm (>40mm) both meet surgical criteria. Waiting for symptoms risks irreversible LV dysfunction. ACEi does not prevent MR deterioration (no evidence for delaying surgery in MR). Surgery: prefer repair over replacement when feasible.
highYield: MR surgery indications: Symptomatic severe MR OR EF <60% OR ESD >40mm. Note: EF cutoff in MR is 60% (not 55% as in other conditions) because EF is overestimated.
pyqNote: MR surgery threshold (EF <60% or ESD >40mm) with the explanation for lower EF cutoff is high-yield NEET PG.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 72-year-old male presents with exertional angina, exertional syncope, and progressive dyspnea. On examination, there is a harsh ejection systolic murmur at the right 2nd intercostal space radiating to the carotids, with a slow-rising carotid pulse (pulsus parvus et tardus). Echo confirms severe AS (AVA 0.8 cm², mean gradient 50 mmHg). What is the prognosis without intervention?
options: Excellent prognosis; medical management is sufficient
Syncope carries 3-year survival of ~50% without intervention
Dyspnea carries 5-year survival of ~80% without intervention
Angina carries 5-year survival of >90% without intervention
explanation: The classic triad of severe AS symptoms (SAD - Syncope, Angina, Dyspnea) carries progressively poor prognosis without AVR: Angina: average survival 5 years. Syncope: average survival 3 years. Dyspnea/Heart failure: average survival 1-2 years (worst prognosis). Pulsus parvus et tardus (slow-rising, low-amplitude carotid pulse) + ejection systolic murmur at aortic area with radiation to carotids + symptoms confirm severe AS. AVA <1 cm² and mean gradient >40 mmHg = severe AS. Valve replacement (AVR or TAVI) is the only effective treatment.
highYield: AS triad: SAD (Syncope 3yr, Angina 5yr, Dyspnea 1-2yr survival). Severe AS: AVA <1cm², mean gradient >40mmHg. Pulsus parvus et tardus on examination.
pyqNote: Survival after symptoms in AS (SAD and prognosis) is repeatedly tested in NEET PG - Dyspnea = worst, Angina = best among the three.

subject: Medicine
stem: An 84-year-old male with severe AS has a Society of Thoracic Surgeons (STS) score of 12% (high surgical risk). He has severe COPD and porcelain aorta. Which intervention is most appropriate?
options: Balloon aortic valvuloplasty as definitive treatment
Medical management with digoxin and diuretics
explanation: TAVI (Transcatheter Aortic Valve Implantation) is indicated for severe symptomatic AS in patients who are inoperable or at high/intermediate surgical risk (STS score >8% = high risk). This patient has: age 84, high STS score (12%), severe COPD, and porcelain aorta (calcified aorta making surgery very hazardous) - all making SAVR extremely high risk. TAVI is performed via femoral artery (transfemoral - preferred if possible) or alternative access (transapical, transaortic). PARTNER trial established TAVI as the standard of care for inoperable AS. Balloon valvuloplasty is only a bridge to TAVI/SAVR, not definitive.
highYield: TAVI indications: severe symptomatic AS + inoperable OR high surgical risk (STS >8%). Porcelain aorta, frailty = favor TAVI. Now expanding to intermediate/low risk.
pyqNote: TAVI indications and the concept of STS risk score are increasingly tested in NEET PG and super-specialty exams.

subject: Medicine
stem: A patient with acute anterior STEMI (V1-V4 elevation) also shows ST depression in leads II, III, and aVF. What is the clinical significance of these inferior ST depressions?
options: They indicate a simultaneous inferior MI requiring additional stenting
They are reciprocal changes of anterior STEMI and do not indicate additional inferior territory involvement
They suggest LBBB masking inferior MI
explanation: Reciprocal changes (ST depression) in leads II, III, aVF during anterior STEMI represent the mirror image of anterior wall ST elevation seen from the opposing inferior electrical perspective. These are TRUE RECIPROCAL changes, NOT evidence of additional inferior MI. They actually carry prognostic significance: larger reciprocal changes may indicate more extensive anterior MI and higher risk. The key to differentiation: in simultaneous anterior + inferior STEMI, you would see BOTH ST elevation in V1-V4 AND ST elevation in II/III/aVF, not depression in inferior leads. Treatment remains focused on opening the LAD.
highYield: Reciprocal ST depression in inferior leads during anterior STEMI = mirror image, NOT additional inferior MI. More reciprocal change = larger MI, higher risk.
pyqNote: Understanding reciprocal changes vs simultaneous MI is a conceptual NEET PG question.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 25-year-old asymptomatic male is found to have WPW pattern on pre-employment ECG (delta wave + short PR + wide QRS). Electrophysiology study shows an accessory pathway with shortest pre-excited RR interval of 220ms during induced AF. What is the recommended management?
options: Reassure and discharge; no treatment needed for asymptomatic WPW
Catheter ablation of the accessory pathway
explanation: In asymptomatic WPW, risk stratification is done by EPS (electrophysiology study). High-risk features on EPS warranting ablation: shortest pre-excited RR interval during AF <250ms (indicates the accessory pathway can conduct rapidly, predisposing to VF), inducible AVRT, multiple accessory pathways, pathway with short effective refractory period. Here, shortest pre-excited RR = 220ms (<250ms) = HIGH RISK → ablation recommended. For truly low-risk asymptomatic WPW (shortest RR >250ms), watchful waiting may be appropriate, but ablation is increasingly offered given near-100% cure rates with low procedural risk.
highYield: WPW ablation: Symptomatic WPW = always ablate. Asymptomatic + shortest pre-excited RR <250ms on EPS = high risk = ablate. Curative in >95% cases.
pyqNote: WPW risk stratification using shortest pre-excited RR interval is an advanced but increasingly tested topic.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 62-year-old diabetic male with HFrEF (EF 35%) is on optimal therapy with sacubitril-valsartan, carvedilol, and spironolactone. His HbA1c is 7.2% (well-controlled). What is the most appropriate addition to further reduce his cardiovascular risk?
explanation: SGLT2 inhibitors (dapagliflozin - DAPA-HF trial; empagliflozin - EMPEROR-Reduced trial) are now the 4th pillar of HFrEF therapy, recommended REGARDLESS of diabetes status. In DAPA-HF, dapagliflozin reduced CV death + worsening HF by 26% in HFrEF patients with and without diabetes. SGLT2i mechanism in HF: osmotic diuresis, natriuresis, reduction of cardiac preload and afterload, improved myocardial energetics. GLP-1 agonists are safe in HF/diabetes but do NOT reduce HF hospitalizations (FIGHT trial - neutral). Metformin is safe in HF but has no cardiac benefit. DPP-4 inhibitors (saxagliptin) may worsen HF.
highYield: SGLT2i = 4th pillar of HFrEF. DAPA-HF (dapagliflozin) and EMPEROR-Reduced (empagliflozin) = reduced HF events regardless of DM status.
pyqNote: SGLT2i as HFrEF therapy (not just for DM) is one of the most important recent updates tested in NEET PG.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 42-year-old female with known mitral stenosis presents with sudden-onset palpitations. ECG shows an irregular rhythm with absent P waves and variable R-R intervals. Ventricular rate is 130 bpm. What is the most common arrhythmic complication of mitral stenosis?
explanation: Atrial fibrillation (AF) is the most common and important arrhythmic complication of mitral stenosis. Chronic left atrial pressure overload in MS leads to LA enlargement and fibrosis, creating a substrate for AF. AF in MS is particularly dangerous because: (1) Loss of atrial kick further reduces CO in a stenotic valve; (2) Rapid ventricular rate reduces diastolic filling time across the stenotic mitral valve, causing acute pulmonary edema; (3) LA stasis in AF + enlarged LA = high risk of thrombus formation and systemic embolism (stroke). Management: Rate control (beta-blocker, digoxin if EF reduced), anticoagulation (warfarin; DOACs have limited data in rheumatic AF), and treat underlying MS.
highYield: MS most common complication = Atrial Fibrillation. Causes: LA enlargement, fibrosis. Consequences: reduced CO, pulmonary edema, thromboembolism/stroke.
pyqNote: AF as complication of MS is standard NEET PG. The mechanism (LA enlargement) and consequences (stroke risk) are tested.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 45-year-old male with chronic aortic regurgitation has a collapsing pulse, wide pulse pressure (180/50 mmHg), and a high-pitched blowing early diastolic murmur at the left sternal edge. Corrigan's pulse, de Musset's sign, and Quincke's sign are present. What is the classic auscultatory finding of significant AR?
options: Austin Flint murmur - a mid-diastolic rumble at the apex
Opening snap followed by mid-diastolic murmur
Pansystolic murmur at the apex radiating to axilla
Ejection click followed by ejection systolic murmur
explanation: Austin Flint murmur is a mid-diastolic (or presystolic) rumble heard at the apex in significant aortic regurgitation. It is caused by the AR jet impinging on the anterior mitral leaflet, causing functional mitral stenosis (the jet partially closes the mitral valve, creating turbulence during diastolic flow). It mimics MS but is distinguished by: absence of loud S1, absence of opening snap, presence of AR signs. Wide pulse pressure (here 130mmHg) and collapsing pulse (water hammer pulse = Corrigan's pulse) are characteristic of AR. Quincke's sign = capillary pulsations in nail bed. De Musset's sign = head nodding with pulse.
highYield: AR clinical signs: Water-hammer pulse (Corrigan's), wide pulse pressure, Austin Flint murmur (functional MS), Quincke's, de Musset's. Early diastolic murmur = AR signature.
pyqNote: Austin Flint murmur mechanism and distinguishing from true MS is a classic NEET PG question.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 68-year-old male with HFrEF (EF 30%) develops a dry persistent cough after starting enalapril. Creatinine and potassium are normal. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Switch to hydralazine + isosorbide dinitrate as permanent alternative
Switch to an ARB (e.g., losartan or candesartan)
Stop all renin-angiotensin system agents permanently
explanation: ACEi (like enalapril) cause dry cough in 10-15% of patients (more common in Asians) due to accumulation of bradykinin (normally broken down by ACE). The most appropriate step is to switch to sacubitril-valsartan (ARNI), which contains valsartan (ARB, no bradykinin accumulation = no cough) combined with sacubitril (neprilysin inhibitor). This provides superior mortality reduction vs ACEi alone (PARADIGM-HF). ARBs (losartan) are an alternative if ARNI is not tolerated or unavailable. Hydralazine + nitrates is reserved for patients who cannot tolerate BOTH ACEi AND ARBs (e.g., bilateral renal artery stenosis, severe hyperkalemia, renal failure, pregnancy). ARNI requires 36-hour washout from ACEi before starting.
highYield: ACEi cough = bradykinin accumulation. Switch to ARNI (sacubitril-valsartan) = best option (superior mortality benefit). ARNI requires 36hr washout from ACEi. ARB = alternative if ARNI unavailable.
pyqNote: ACEi cough management and the superiority of ARNI over ARB alone is a high-yield NEET PG topic.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 35-year-old female with known WPW syndrome presents with sudden palpitations. ECG shows an irregular wide-complex tachycardia with varying QRS morphology, ventricular rate 220 bpm. There is no AV dissociation. What is the most dangerous diagnosis and treatment?
options: AVNRT with aberrant conduction - treat with adenosine
Pre-excited atrial fibrillation (WPW + AF) - treat with IV procainamide
Atrial flutter with variable block - treat with diltiazem
explanation: Irregular wide-complex tachycardia with varying QRS morphology in a patient with WPW = Pre-excited AF. Impulses from AF bypass the AV node via the accessory pathway, conducting at very high rates (>200 bpm) directly to the ventricles, causing an irregular wide-complex tachycardia with varying QRS morphology (delta waves of varying width). This can degenerate into VF and cause sudden cardiac death. AV nodal blocking agents (Adenosine, Digoxin, Verapamil, Beta-blockers) are ABSOLUTELY CONTRAINDICATED: they block the AV node, leaving the accessory pathway as the only conduction route, accelerating ventricular rate → VF. Treatment: DC cardioversion if hemodynamically unstable; IV Procainamide or Ibutilide if stable. Definitive: accessory pathway ablation.
highYield: Pre-excited AF in WPW = irregular wide-complex tachycardia. Risk of VF. NEVER give adenosine/digoxin/verapamil/beta-blockers. Give IV Procainamide. Ablate accessory pathway.
pyqNote: Pre-excited AF in WPW and the contraindication of AV nodal blockers is one of the most important and repeatedly tested NEET PG emergency cardiology topics.

subject: Medicine
topic: Valvular Disease - Mitral Stenosis Murmur Features
stem: A 38-year-old female with rheumatic heart disease has a mid-diastolic rumbling murmur best heard at the cardiac apex with the bell of the stethoscope, with the patient in the left lateral decubitus position. There is an opening snap. In which position/maneuver does this murmur typically INCREASE?
options: Left lateral decubitus position with bell of stethoscope
explanation: Mitral stenosis murmur (mid-diastolic rumble) is best heard with: Patient in LEFT LATERAL DECUBITUS position (brings the LV apex closer to the chest wall), using the BELL of the stethoscope (low-frequency murmur) at the cardiac apex, after MILD EXERCISE (increases heart rate and blood flow across the valve, accentuates murmur). Maneuvers that increase LV filling (squatting, raising legs) increase MS murmur. Valsalva and standing DECREASE venous return and reduce murmur. Right-sided murmurs increase with inspiration (Carvallo's sign). MS murmur is a left-sided low-frequency murmur.
highYield: MS murmur best heard: Left lateral decubitus + bell + cardiac apex + after mild exercise. Increases with increased venous return (squatting, leg raise).
pyqNote: Auscultatory technique for MS murmur is a standard clinical NEET PG question.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 58-year-old male with HFrEF (EF 28%), already on sacubitril-valsartan and carvedilol, has serum creatinine of 1.6 mg/dL and potassium of 5.3 mEq/L. You plan to add spironolactone. Which is a CONTRAINDICATION to adding spironolactone?
explanation: Spironolactone (MRA - Mineralocorticoid Receptor Antagonist) is the 3rd pillar of HFrEF therapy (RALES trial). Contraindications to spironolactone in HF: Serum potassium >5.0 mEq/L (risk of life-threatening hyperkalemia, especially combined with ACEi/ARB/ARNI); eGFR <30 mL/min (or creatinine >2.5 mg/dL in men, >2.0 in women); concurrent use with other potassium-sparing diuretics. This patient has K+ 5.3 mEq/L (>5.0) - CONTRAINDICATED. The creatinine of 1.6 mg/dL alone does not contraindicate use. Monitor K+ and renal function closely if initiated. Eplerenone is an alternative with less gynecomastia.
highYield: Spironolactone contraindications in HF: K+ >5.0 mEq/L, creatinine >2.5 mg/dL (men), eGFR <30. Monitor K+ and renal function after initiation.
pyqNote: Spironolactone contraindications (especially hyperkalemia threshold) are tested in NEET PG pharmacology and medicine.

subject: Medicine
topic: ECG - Wellens Type A vs Type B
stem: A 50-year-old male presents to the ER with chest pain that resolved 3 hours ago. ECG now shows biphasic T waves in V2-V3 (initially positive, then negative). Troponin is borderline elevated. Which statement is most accurate?
options: This represents normal T-wave morphology in V2-V3
This is Wellens Type A pattern representing critical proximal LAD stenosis; stress testing is safe
This is Wellens Type A pattern (biphasic T) indicating critical proximal LAD stenosis; stress testing is contraindicated
This is de Winter T-wave pattern requiring immediate PCI
explanation: Wellens syndrome has two patterns, both indicating critical proximal LAD stenosis in pain-free or post-pain state: Type A (Pattern 1): Biphasic T waves in V2-V3 (positive-negative biphasic T, small initial positive deflection followed by terminal negative deflection) - less common but equally dangerous. Type B (Pattern 2): Deep, symmetric T-wave inversions in V2-V3 - more common (75%). Both patterns: occur in PAIN-FREE state (patient appears well), indicate critical stenosis of proximal LAD likely to cause massive anterior MI, represent spontaneous reperfusion pattern. STRESS TEST IS ABSOLUTELY CONTRAINDICATED (can trigger complete occlusion). Urgent coronary angiography is indicated. De Winter T waves show upsloping ST depression + tall T waves (different pattern).
highYield: Wellens Type A = biphasic T (positive-negative) in V2-V3. Type B = deep symmetric T inversion V2-V3. Both = critical LAD stenosis. Both = stress test CONTRAINDICATED.
pyqNote: Differentiating Wellens Type A (biphasic) vs Type B (deep symmetric T inversion) and the contraindication of stress test is high-yield.
